questions.question: 中文导读：Why do pose models regress heatmaps instead of (x, y) coordinates directly?
options: 中文选项：Heatmaps are cheaper to compute

中文选项：The spatial structure of a conv feature map aligns with spatial output; a Gaussian heatmap target provides a smooth loss landscape that tolerates small localisation errors, whereas direct coordinate regression is brittle and loses spatial context
中文选项：Heatmaps are required by the COCO metric
中文选项：Coordinate regression produces NaN
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Regressing coordinates with MSE asks the network to reduce a 2D position to two scalars, losing the feature-map alignment that CNNs exploit. Heatmap regression gives the network a per-pixel loss that is smooth around the true location and preserves spatial priors. The empirical improvement over coordinate regression is large enough that every modern pose model uses heatmaps.

questions.question: 中文导读：Top-down pose estimation vs bottom-up: which scales better with crowd size, and why?
options: 中文选项：Top-down, because each person uses a separate fast model
中文选项：Bottom-up, because it does one forward pass over the whole image then groups keypoints, so runtime is constant in the number of people
中文选项：They scale equally
中文选项：Top-down never scales
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Top-down runs a per-person keypoint model after a person detector, so cost grows linearly with the number of people. Bottom-up (OpenPose, HigherHRNet) produces all keypoints and association fields in one pass, then groups them — constant time regardless of crowd density. The trade: top-down is more accurate per-person; bottom-up is faster in crowds.

questions.question: 中文导读：What are Part Affinity Fields?
options: 中文选项：A scheduling algorithm

中文选项：2-channel unit-vector fields that encode the direction from one keypoint to another; integrating the PAF along a candidate line tells you whether two keypoints belong to the same instance, enabling bottom-up association without per-person detection
中文选项：A data augmentation technique
中文选项：A type of loss function
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Per connected keypoint pair (limb), predict a 2-channel field (x, y components of the unit vector pointing from one keypoint to the other). To match a candidate shoulder with a candidate elbow, integrate the PAF along the line joining them; higher integral = stronger match. This turns pose into a bipartite matching problem solvable in polynomial time.

questions.question: 中文导读：Why does sub-pixel refinement around the argmax meaningfully lift keypoint accuracy?
options: 中文选项：It smooths the heatmap

中文选项：Integer argmax rounds to the nearest grid cell; fitting a local parabola or using the offset dx = 0.25*(heatmap[y,x+1] - heatmap[y,x-1]) recovers the continuous peak position, often halving the L2 error for cleanly predicted keypoints
中文选项：It prevents overfitting
中文选项：It normalises the output
questions.explanation: 中文解析：A well-predicted heatmap has a smooth Gaussian peak whose centre is usually between grid cells. Integer argmax loses that sub-pixel information (up to 0.5 px error). Fitting a parabola or using the first-difference offset recovers the continuous peak. For sports analytics, medical landmarks, or anything requiring precise coordinates, this step is mandatory.

questions.question: 中文导读：OKS (Object Keypoint Similarity) is the pose-estimation analogue of what object-detection metric?
options: 中文选项：Inference latency
中文选项：IoU — both measure geometric match between prediction and ground truth, with OKS using keypoint distances weighted by each keypoint's annotation variance; COCO reports mAP@OKS 0.5:0.95 for pose
中文选项：Classification accuracy
中文选项：Cross-entropy loss
questions.explanation: 中文解析：OKS ranges 0 to 1 like IoU and plays the same role: it decides whether a prediction matches a ground-truth pose at a given strictness level. Each keypoint has a variance (COCO publishes them) that scales its contribution — invariant joints like nose and eyes weigh more than wrists, which are annotated less consistently. COCO Pose AP @ OKS 0.5:0.95 is the 2026 community benchmark.
